questions.question: Which AWS service is the native store for **traces** (the causal path of one request across services as a tree of segments/subsegments)?
options: AWS X-Ray
CloudWatch Logs
CloudWatch Metrics
Amazon EventBridge
questions.explanation: Traces live in AWS X-Ray (or any OTel backend via ADOT). CloudWatch Metrics holds aggregated numbers and CloudWatch Logs holds discrete timestamped events; EventBridge is an event bus, not an observability store.

questions.question: A CloudWatch metric is uniquely identified by which combination?
options: Namespace + metric name + the full set of dimensions
Metric name + region only
Namespace + a single primary dimension
Metric name + statistic + period
questions.explanation: Identity is namespace + name + the complete dimension set; querying a different dimension combination is a different metric. Statistic and period are query-time aggregations, not part of the metric's identity.

questions.question: What is the storage granularity of a **standard-resolution** CloudWatch metric versus a **high-resolution** custom metric?
options: Standard = 1 minute; high-resolution = 1 second
Standard = 5 minutes; high-resolution = 1 minute
Standard = 1 second; high-resolution = 1 millisecond
Standard = 1 minute; high-resolution = 100 milliseconds
questions.explanation: Standard resolution stores at 1-minute granularity; high-resolution custom metrics store at 1-second granularity (at extra cost). 5-minute vs 1-minute describes basic vs detailed EC2 monitoring, not metric resolution.

questions.question: For a user-facing latency SLO, why alarm on **p99** rather than `Average`?
options: The average is dominated by fast requests and hides the slow tail that p99 exposes
Average cannot be used as an alarm statistic in CloudWatch
p99 is cheaper to compute and store than Average
Average requires high-resolution metrics while p99 does not
questions.explanation: An average is pulled down by the bulk of fast requests, so a static-threshold Average alarm looks healthy while the slowest 1% time out; p99 reflects what those users actually experience. Average is a perfectly valid alarm statistic and is not inherently more expensive.

questions.question: CloudWatch Events and Amazon EventBridge are two separate products that must be wired together.
questions.explanation: They are the same service; EventBridge is simply the current name for what used to be called CloudWatch Events. Treating them as two distinct products is a known trap.

questions.question: A high-throughput .NET Lambda already writes structured JSON logs and needs to emit an `OrdersPlaced` count and `LatencyMs` per invocation **without** adding latency or an extra API call on the hot path, keeping each metric correlated to its log line. What should it use?
options: Embedded Metric Format (EMF): one structured log line CloudWatch extracts from
A synchronous `PutMetricData` API call on every single invocation
A metric filter applied after the fact to the function's log group
A subscription filter forwarding matching events to Kinesis Data Streams
questions.explanation: EMF emits one structured log line from which CloudWatch extracts the metric asynchronously — no hot-path API call, no added latency, and the metric shares dimensions/properties with its log line. `PutMetricData` adds a synchronous call per invocation; metric/subscription filters are downstream of logs and don't solve the per-invocation emission.

questions.question: What is the key difference between a CloudWatch Logs **metric filter** and a **subscription filter**?
options: A metric filter produces an alarmable CloudWatch metric; a subscription filter streams raw events to Lambda/Kinesis/Firehose/OpenSearch
A metric filter streams events to OpenSearch; a subscription filter produces a CloudWatch metric you can alarm on
Both produce metrics, but the subscription filter does it in real time and the metric filter only in batch
A metric filter forwards raw events to SNS; a subscription filter forwards raw events to SQS
questions.explanation: A metric filter distills matching log patterns into an alarmable metric; a subscription filter fans raw matching events out to Lambda, Kinesis Data Streams, Firehose, or OpenSearch. They are not interchangeable — one makes a number, the other forwards events.

questions.question: A p99 latency alarm pages constantly during predictable 9 a.m. and 5 p.m. traffic peaks (users are fine) yet misses a real midnight regression. Which alarm type best fixes this?
options: Anomaly-detection alarm
Static-threshold alarm with more evaluation periods
Composite alarm over the latency alarm
A high-resolution version of the same static alarm
questions.explanation: An anomaly-detection alarm trains a band from history and fires when the metric leaves it, handling daily/weekly seasonality that a single static threshold gets wrong at both peak and trough. A composite alarm reduces storms but still depends on a poorly-fitting underlying threshold.

questions.question: How does a **composite alarm** reduce alert fatigue when a latency alarm, an error-rate alarm, and an unhealthy-host alarm all fire together during one outage?
options: It notifies only when a boolean combination of the child alarms signals a real incident, and can suppress the children's own notifications
It deletes the child alarms automatically once any incident is detected and confirmed
It increases each child alarm's evaluation periods so the children fire far less often
It routes every child alarm's notification through one SNS topic without changing when they fire
questions.explanation: A composite alarm is a boolean expression over other alarms (e.g. ALARM(latency) AND ALARM(errors) AND ALARM(unhealthyHosts)); it pages once when the combination indicates a real incident and can mute the children while it is active. It does not delete children or merely re-route their independent notifications.

questions.question: During an incident you must find only the X-Ray traces from gold-tier customers that hit a fault, using a filter expression like `annotation.customerTier = "gold" AND fault`. How should `customerTier` be recorded on the segment?
options: As an annotation, because annotations are indexed and filterable
As metadata, because metadata supports arbitrary JSON
As a high-cardinality metric dimension
As a subsegment name so the service map can group it
questions.explanation: Only annotations are indexed/searchable, so filter expressions can query them; metadata rides along for context but is not searchable. Annotations are limited to strings/numbers/booleans, which `customerTier` satisfies.

questions.question: Given the X-Ray sampling rule below plus a default rule of `{ "fixed_target": 1, "rate": 0.05 }`, what happens to `/checkout` requests?
options: Every `/checkout` request is sampled, because the higher-priority rule matches and its rate is 1.0
Only about 5% of `/checkout` requests are sampled, because the default rule overrides named rules
`/checkout` requests are never sampled, because two rules conflict and cancel out
Exactly 2 `/checkout` requests per second are sampled and the rest are dropped
questions.explanation: The lower priority number wins and the first matching rule applies, so `/checkout*` matches the priority-100 rule with `rate: 1.0` and is always sampled (the `fixed_target` reservoir of 2/sec is captured first, then 100% of the remainder). The default 5% rule applies only to non-matching paths.

questions.question: Inside a Lambda function, how do you obtain the active X-Ray trace id so you can log it as a correlation id that joins logs to traces?
options: Read the `_X_AMZN_TRACE_ID` environment variable
Read the `X-Amzn-Trace-Id` value from the SNS message body
Call `PutMetricData` and read the returned trace id
Parse the W3C `traceparent` from the Lambda response headers
questions.explanation: Lambda exposes the active trace id via the `_X_AMZN_TRACE_ID` environment variable; logging it ties the function's logs to its trace. `X-Amzn-Trace-Id` is the propagation header on HTTP hops, not something pulled from an SNS body, and `PutMetricData` returns no trace id.

questions.question: Which statements about the **unified CloudWatch agent** on EC2 are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: It is required to collect in-guest memory and disk-used metrics, which the hypervisor cannot see from outside
It collects both host metrics and logs from a single agent and config file
It can collect StatsD and collectd metrics and emit EMF
Enabling detailed EC2 monitoring alone reports in-guest memory and disk-used without the agent
It only works on EC2 and cannot run on-premises or in containers
questions.explanation: The unified agent is one daemon/config that collects host metrics and logs, supports StatsD/collectd and EMF, and is the only way to get guest-OS memory/disk metrics. Detailed monitoring just makes existing metrics 1-minute (not in-guest memory), and the agent also runs on-prem and in containers.

questions.question: What distinguishes CloudWatch **Synthetics canaries** from **RUM**?
options: Canaries are proactive scripted checks on a schedule; RUM is real-user browser telemetry
Canaries report telemetry from real users; RUM runs scripted checks on a schedule
Both are proactive synthetic checks, differing only in language (Node vs Python)
Canaries collect traces while RUM collects metrics, but neither involves real users
questions.explanation: Synthetics canaries proactively exercise an endpoint from outside before a real user hits a broken path; RUM is reactive/real, measuring what actual users experienced via browser JS. The proactive-vs-real distinction is the discriminator.

questions.question: After a deploy, log ingestion is flat but the custom-metric count exploded and the bill jumped. In this EMF snippet, which change most likely caused it?
options: `OrderId` was promoted into a dimension, so every unique order id became a billable metric
`PutMetric` was called with milliseconds instead of count, doubling the metric charge
Two `SetDimensions` calls are not allowed and silently duplicate every metric
Adding a second dimension set disables EMF extraction, forcing fallback to `PutMetricData`
questions.explanation: Cardinality is the cost driver: every unique combination of dimension values is a separate billable custom metric, so putting a high-cardinality `OrderId` into a dimension multiplies metrics. High-cardinality ids belong in EMF properties (searchable in logs), not dimensions; the unit and multiple dimension sets are not the cause.

questions.question: A new microservice must export to X-Ray today but the org may switch to a third-party tracing backend next year. Which statements support choosing **OpenTelemetry + the ADOT Collector** over the X-Ray SDK + daemon? (Select all that apply.)
options: ADOT is OpenTelemetry-standard, so swapping backends is a collector-config change rather than a code rewrite
One ADOT Collector can ingest traces, metrics, and logs and export to X-Ray, CloudWatch, AMP, or third parties
The X-Ray daemon is trace-only and X-Ray-proprietary, locking instrumentation to X-Ray
The X-Ray daemon can export OTLP to any third-party backend, so it is equally portable
ADOT cannot export to X-Ray, so it forces an immediate migration away from AWS
questions.explanation: ADOT is the vendor-neutral, multi-signal, multi-backend path, so a backend swap is a config change; the X-Ray daemon is trace-only, X-Ray-proprietary, and not OTLP-exporting. ADOT does export to X-Ray today, so no forced migration.

questions.question: CloudWatch can compute a meaningful p99 from a custom metric that was published as only a pre-rolled `Average` value with no underlying distribution or sample count.
questions.explanation: Percentiles require CloudWatch to retain the underlying distribution; they are supported when metrics are published with full data points or with `StatisticValues` (which carries sample count), but not when only a pre-rolled `Average` is available.

questions.question: A fleet mixes services that propagate the X-Ray `X-Amzn-Trace-Id` header with services that propagate W3C `traceparent`, and traces break at the boundary between them. What is the correct senior fix?
options: Standardize on one propagator, using ADOT to translate between W3C and X-Ray ids
Disable sampling so every request is captured and the broken edges disappear
Move all annotations to metadata so the trace ids stop conflicting
Switch every service to high-resolution metrics to align ids on a 1-second boundary
questions.explanation: Mixed propagators break traces at the boundary; the fix is to choose one propagator consistently, and ADOT can translate between W3C trace context and X-Ray ids so mixed fleets interoperate. Sampling, annotations/metadata, and metric resolution are unrelated to header propagation.
